claim: Hand B text statistics show the same section-independence pattern as Hand A: no significant correlation between section/quire assignment and text metrics (TTR, mean length, Layer-2 outer usage, marker frequency). If confirmed, the generative-process-overlay interpretation extends manuscript-wide — both hands produce section-independent text. If refuted, Hand B differs from Hand A and may encode section-specific content.
evidence_for: H-BV-EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01 established section-independence for Hand A on 4 of 4 section-correlation tests (C1-C4 all p > 0.01). The question is whether Hand B exhibits the same pattern.

H-BV-HAND-B-MARKOV-01 CONFIRMED that Hand B shares Hand A's Markov-1-flat character-level signature. If the generative process is shared between hands, and Hand A's output is section-independent, Hand B's output would be expected to be section-independent too.

Hand B has BETTER section balance than Hand A: 32 herbal / 23 recipes / 19 biological / 5 text-only / 3 cosmological among folios with >=30 tokens. Statistical power for inter-section ANOVA is substantially higher than Hand A (which was 95 herbal dominated).
Hand B's most frequent token 'chedy' (491 occurrences) is an ideal marker for frequency-per-section testing, analogous to Hand A's 'daiin' use. The pre-reg locks chedy as the marker before execution.
evidence_against: Hand B differs from Hand A in several ways: longer mean word length (4.47 vs 4.24), steeper Zipf slope (1.06 vs 0.97), more repetitive TTR (0.34 vs 0.36). These are parameter-level differences under the shared generative-process reading. Section correlations could differ parametrically — Hand B might show section-specific effects that Hand A masks or vice versa.

Hand B's section composition includes biological folios (19) that Hand A doesn't have at the tested scale. If biological content is statistically different from recipes/herbal at the text level, Hand B may show section effects that Hand A couldn't have shown regardless of underlying structure.
Multiple-testing and shuffle-null design are identical to EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01. C8 (word_count x TTR) remains a likely sampling artefact and should be flagged.

A Hand B CONFIRMED result (section-independence) extends the generative-process-overlay narrative. A Hand B REFUTED result (section-specificity) requires a more complex reading: same generative process but B is section-aware while A isn't.
test:
LOCKED PROCEDURE.

  DATA: Hand B folios from voynich-corpus.json filtered to currier_language=='B' AND word_count >= 30.

  EXTERNAL FEATURES (per folio) — same as EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01:
    F1 Section, F2 Quire, F3 Folio linear position, F4 Word count, F5 Line count.

  TEXT STATISTICS (per folio) — same metric definitions, Hand B marker:
    T1 TTR: distinct types / total tokens
    T2 Mean token length: mean character length across tokens
    T3 Layer-2 outer usage: count of {y, n, r, ol, l} appearing in folio (0-5)
    T4 chedy frequency: count of 'chedy' tokens / total tokens in folio (analogous to Hand A's daiin test)

  EIGHT LOCKED CORRELATIONS (identical structure to EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01):
    C1 Section x TTR (ANOVA)
    C2 Section x mean_length (ANOVA)
    C3 Section x L2_outer_usage (ANOVA)
    C4 Section x chedy_freq (ANOVA)
    C5 Quire x TTR (ANOVA)
    C6 Position x TTR (Spearman)
    C7 Position x mean_length (Spearman)
    C8 Word_count x TTR (Spearman) — flagged as likely sampling artefact per methodology lesson 8

  NULL DISTRIBUTION: 1000 shuffles per correlation, same seed offsets as EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01.

  DECISION RULE (LOCKED):
    CONFIRMED: Hand B section correlations (C1-C4) show >= 3 of 4 non-significant at p < 0.01 (matching Hand A's 4/4 non-significant pattern, with one allowed significant to accommodate Hand B's better power).
    REFUTED: Hand B section correlations show >= 2 of 4 significant at p < 0.01 (different from Hand A; Hand B has section-specific content the methodology detects).
    PARTIAL: exactly 2 of 4 section correlations fall in the p ∈ [0.01, 0.05] range (borderline).

    SECONDARY: count total significant across all 8 correlations. Compare to Hand A's 2/8. If Hand B also gets 2/8 with the same structural pattern (C7 significant, C8 artefact), the manuscript-wide overlay reading is reinforced. If Hand B gets substantially more or fewer, the reading shifts.

    COMPARISON TO HAND A (explicit): report a side-by-side table of the 8 p-values for Hand A vs Hand B. Note any correlation where the two hands DIFFER in significance.

  REPORTING: same format as EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01 plus Hand A vs Hand B comparison table.
test_metric: 8 correlations on 82-ish Hand B folios with >=30 tokens. Null: 1000 shuffles per correlation. Decision rule focuses on section correlations C1-C4 to match the primary Hand A finding.
test_threshold: CONFIRMED if >=3 of 4 section correlations non-sig at p<0.01 (matching Hand A pattern). REFUTED if >=2 of 4 section significant. PARTIAL otherwise.

correlation_results.note: Hand B TTR differs strongly by section (F=61). Hand A p was 0.017 (close miss). Hand B's better section balance reveals a real section effect.
Same null result in both hands: Layer-2 outer usage does not differ by section. Outer morphemes remain section-invariant.
Hand B chedy frequency DIFFERS by section. Hand A daiin frequency does not. This is a DIRECT contradiction of the manuscript-wide overlay hypothesis.
Hand B TTR tracks QUIRE grouping strongly. Hand A does not. Another sharp Hand-B-only structural signal.
Hand A has clear +0.36 manuscript-wide length drift. Hand B has weak negative rho (-0.19) that is not significant. The drift is Hand-A-specific.
Sampling artefact in both hands per methodology lesson 8 (TTR is sample-size-dependent).

tests_run.contradicts_parent_hypothesis_finding: Hand A was section-INDEPENDENT (EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01 found 0/4 section sig). Hand B is section-SENSITIVE (2/4 section sig with large F statistics). The two hands show OPPOSITE section-correlation patterns.

verdict_note: REFUTED. Hand B is NOT section-independent like Hand A. On the 4 locked section correlations, Hand B shows 2 significant at Bonferroni-corrected p < 0.00125 (C1 Section x TTR with F=61, and C4 Section x chedy_freq with F=4.4). Hand B ALSO shows significant quire x TTR (C5, F=33, p<0.0001) that Hand A does not. Hand A's manuscript-wide length drift (C7 rho=+0.36) does NOT appear in Hand B. The two hands exhibit OPPOSITE correlation patterns with external features: Hand A is overlay-like (section-independent + linear drift); Hand B is section-sensitive + quire-sensitive + no linear drift. C3 Section x L2_outer_usage is null in BOTH hands (outer morphemes remain the one structural invariant across sections and hands). Implication: the 'generative-process overlay' manuscript-wide narrative needs significant revision. The two hands are not just parameter variants of the same encoding — Hand B's output responds to section and quire in ways Hand A's does not.
findings_for_followup: CRITICAL FOR v2 — NARRATIVE REVISION: Hand B is section-sensitive in a way Hand A is not. The 'generative-process overlay' interpretation from H-BV-EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01 does NOT extend manuscript-wide as hypothesised. The two hands exhibit fundamentally different correlation patterns with external features, suggesting different encoding regimes or different relationships between text and content.

STRONGEST HAND B SIGNAL: C1 Section x TTR F=61 p<0.0001 AND C4 Section x chedy_freq F=4.4 p<0.0001. Hand B's vocabulary diversity AND most-frequent-token usage BOTH vary by section. This is direct evidence of section-content interaction — something Hand A lacks entirely.

INVARIANT ACROSS HANDS: C3 Section x L2_outer_usage is null in BOTH hands (p=0.48 for B, 0.96 for A). Layer-2 outer morphemes {y, n, r, ol, l} are used at section-invariant rates in both hands. This is the one structural feature genuinely shared at the section level — suggesting outers are something fundamentally manuscript-level, not content-level.

HAND A-SPECIFIC (not shared): C7 Position x mean_length. Hand A has a strong linear drift in mean token length (ρ=+0.36, p<0.001); Hand B does not (ρ=-0.19, p=0.09). The manuscript-wide length drift observed in Hand A is actually Hand-A-specific. Combined with Hand B being section-sensitive, this may suggest Hand A and Hand B were authored/produced under materially different conditions.

HAND B-SPECIFIC (not shared): C5 Quire x TTR. Hand B TTR varies by quire (F=33, p<0.0001); Hand A does not (p=0.50). Quire grouping is meaningful for Hand B vocabulary diversity but not Hand A's. This could reflect Hand B being written in chunks aligned with quire structure while Hand A was written more continuously.

THEORY IMPLICATIONS: four candidate readings survive:
      (a) Hand A is generative overlay; Hand B is closer to natural language — 'mixed manuscript' reading
      (b) Both hands are generative but with different context-sensitivity parameters — Hand A ignores section, Hand B conditions on it
      (c) Same generator applied to different input — Hand A's 'input' was section-invariant; Hand B's was section-variant
      (d) Different authors/scribes with different encoding conventions — Currier A/B is not just statistical, but methodological
    All four preserve the MARKOV-1-FLAT character signature (HAND-B-MARKOV-01 CONFIRMED manuscript-wide). The distinction is at the level of how the generative process relates to content.

FOR v2: H-BV-EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01's 'section-independent' framing must be qualified as Hand-A-only. The manuscript-wide narrative becomes: 'the character-level Markov-flat signature is shared; the section-level correlation patterns are hand-specific; Hand B has section-content coupling Hand A lacks'. This is a MORE COMPLEX result than either pure generative-overlay OR pure natural-language reading supports.

OPEN QUESTION: what explains the asymmetry? Candidates to test: (1) Hand B's longer mean word length (4.47 vs 4.24) may give its tokens more category information, allowing sectional differentiation that Hand A's shorter tokens can't carry. (2) The biological section (19 Hand B folios, 0 Hand A folios at threshold) may carry substantially different content that drives Hand B's section effect — removing biological and re-running would test this. (3) Hand B may use a more productive morpheme inventory that varies by content.

METHODOLOGY LESSON 9 CANDIDATE: when testing whether a property extends from one corpus to another, always run the parallel test — same metrics, same thresholds, same decision rule. Don't assume extension. This test's REFUTED verdict (instead of the hypothesised CONFIRMED) is a concrete example of why parallel testing matters.
reasoning: H-BV-EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01 found Hand A is section-independent on 4 of 4 section correlations. H-BV-HAND-B-MARKOV-01 found Hand B shares Hand A's character-level Markov-flat signature. The natural extension: does Hand B also show section-independence? If yes, the 'generative-process overlay' reading is manuscript-wide. If no, Hand B differs from Hand A in how it relates to section content. Hand B has better section balance than Hand A (32/23/19/5/3 vs 95/16/2/1/0 across sections with >=30 tokens), giving this test more power to detect section effects if they exist. The pre-reg locks chedy as the Hand B marker (analogous to Hand A's daiin) and uses identical methodology and thresholds as EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01 for direct comparability.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-17 user-directed parallel test of H-BV-EXTERNAL-CORRELATION-01 on Hand B, following H-BV-HAND-B-MARKOV-01 CONFIRMED_MARKOV_B.
